data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was a synchronous institutional failure caused by the breakdown of the 'gift-exchange' diplomatic network that stabilized the Late Bronze Age great powers' club, leaving peripheral palace-states unable to manage subsistence crises.", "mechanism": "The great powers (Hatti, Egypt, Mitanni, Babylon) maintained stability through a system of reciprocal gift-giving, dynastic marriage, and managed trade that trickled down to vassal states. When the Hittite-Egyptian rivalry ended with the peace treaty, the system's competitive drive faltered. Subsequent disruption of this elite network by internal Hittite succession crises severed the flow of prestige goods and emergency grain shipments to dependent peripheries like Ugarit and Mycenaean Greece. Without these buffers, localized harvest failures triggered elite infighting, administrative collapse, and eventual abandonment.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria", "Babylon"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows that Mycenaean and Ugaritic granaries were full and trade in bulk commodities continued unabated until the final destruction.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The Hittite state collapsed before Ugarit, and Ugarit's final texts show it was still receiving Hittite directives and military support.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This account shifts causality from materialist factors (drought, iron) to the fragility of an international political economy. It explains why Egypt survived (it was a net grain exporter and core member, not a dependent periphery) and why the collapse was simultaneous (the network was tightly coupled). It is vulnerable to evidence that the peripheries were more autarkic than assumed."}
